statement: International aviation emissions within the European Economic Area fall under the EU Emissions Trading System. Aviation was included in the EU ETS by Directive 2008/101/EC of 19 November 2008, amending Directive 2003/87/EC. The original 2008 legislation was designed to cover CO2 emissions from all flights departing from, arriving at, or within the EEA. However, due to international opposition, the scope was temporarily reduced by Regulation (EU) No 421/2014 to cover only intra-EEA flights (the 'reduced scope' or 'stop the clock' derogation), and this restriction has been repeatedly extended. Following the 2023 revision (Directive (EU) 2023/958), the EU ETS for aviation continues to apply to all intra-EEA flights. The limitation to intra-EEA scope was extended one final time until the start of 2027; by July 2026, the European Commission will assess whether to expand coverage to flights departing the EEA to third countries not participating in ICAO's CORSIA scheme. The aviation ETS cap was initially set at 95% of 2004-2006 average emissions (approximately 221.4 million allowances). Aircraft operators were originally allocated 82% of allowances for free, with 15% auctioned and 3% held as a special reserve for new entrants.
source_name: European Commission — Reducing emissions from aviation; Directive 2008/101/EC; Directive (EU) 2023/958
caveats: The scope of aviation in the EU ETS has changed multiple times since 2008 and may change again after the July 2026 assessment. The characterisation of intra-EEA aviation emissions as falling 'under the ETS trading system' is accurate for flights between and within EEA states, but flights to/from non-EEA third countries are currently excluded (subject to review). CORSIA (Carbon Offsetting and Reduction Scheme for International Aviation) applies to qualifying international flights not covered by the EU ETS.

related_entries.statement: Iceland participates in the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) through the EEA Agreement, not through a separate bilateral agreement. The EU ETS Directive (2003/87/EC) was incorporated into the EEA Agreement through EEA Joint Committee Decision No 146/2007 of 26 October 2007, which inserted the Directive as point 21al of Annex XX (Environment) to the EEA Agreement. Constitutional requirements were indicated by Iceland, Liechtenstein, and Norway; these were fulfilled by 28 December 2007, and the decision entered into force on 29 December 2007. Iceland, Norway, and Liechtenstein have participated in the EU ETS since 1 January 2008 (the start of Phase II) on the same legal basis as EU Member States. Subsequent amendments to the ETS Directive — including the aviation inclusion (Directive 2008/101/EC), the Phase III reforms, and the Fit for 55 revisions — have also been incorporated into the EEA Agreement through further Joint Committee Decisions amending Annex XX. The EEA Agreement framework means that the EFTA Surveillance Authority (ESA) monitors compliance in the EEA EFTA states, while the EFTA Court has jurisdiction over disputes — as demonstrated by the EFTA Court judgment in Case E-2/25 (November 2025) concerning ETS obligations.

Free allocation of EU ETS allowances to aircraft operators is being phased out under the Fit for 55 reforms adopted in 2023. Under the revised Directive 2003/87/EC, free allocation to aviation is reduced by 25% in 2024, by 50% in 2025, and eliminated entirely from 2026 onwards for EU/EEA operators generally. Correspondingly, the auctioning share increases. From 2024, the aviation cap is also reduced annually by applying a linear reduction factor increased from 2.2% to 4.3% for 2024-2027 (and 4.4% from 2028). The description of free allowances as 'subsidising' airline emissions, while not technically precise (allowances are not a direct subsidy but a regulatory allocation), reflects the economic reality that airlines receiving free allowances avoid purchasing them at market price, reducing their cost of compliance. At ETS carbon prices of EUR 50-80 per tonne (typical 2023-2025 range), free allocation represents a substantial implicit benefit. The phase-out of free allocation is intended to ensure that the aviation sector bears its full carbon cost, in line with the polluter-pays principle.

Iceland negotiated a special arrangement to continue receiving free EU ETS aviation allowances beyond the standard phase-out schedule. In May 2023, at the Council of Europe summit in Reykjavik, Iceland and the European Commission reached a preliminary agreement for Icelandic aircraft operators to continue receiving free EU ETS emissions allowances up to and including the year 2026. This is one year beyond the general EU/EEA phase-out (where free allocation drops to zero from 2026). The Icelandic government argued that the standard rules harm the competitive position of Icelandic airlines and fail to account for Iceland's geographical isolation, which makes its citizens more dependent on air transport than other EEA residents. The EU sought to grant the Icelandic aviation sector an extra year of free allowances as a special solution compatible with the EU's broader climate goals. The agreement was subject to approval by EU Member States, the Icelandic government, and the Icelandic parliament (Althingi). Claims that the exemption extends to 'end of 2027' appear to conflate two different things: Iceland's free allocation exemption (through 2026) and the broader EU ETS aviation geographical scope limitation to intra-EEA flights (extended until start of 2027).
